Having multiple ways to collect notes is useful because it allows me to:

1. Expand collections easily over time (e.g. moving blocks to a dedicated page)
2. Curate collections by linking or unlinking references
3. Add to collections while preserving context

In Roam, you can create collections of notes (i.e. blocks) by adding them:

a) under parent blocks
b) on pages
c) as block references, or
d) to linked/unlinked page references

References also allow you to add notes to multiple, overlapping collections. https://t.co/5XyuzEZPne

Metaphor #5: [[Collections]]

This is the reigning metaphor for TfT tools.

Notes filed in folders.
Notes grouped with tags.
Notes connected via links. https://t.co/TH7hM4MfCZ

Most of the points are from major categories: Science, Art, Religion, etc. I just had ChatGPT give me a list of the top 10-15 events in those categories.

As I add events from my interests and study, I will look for how they relate and connect with those key events in history.

The practical benefits I've noticed so far:

1. In the linked references for a person, I get a personalized timeline
2. When I block reference an event, the block outline gives me the the historical context
3. Zooming in on a century helps me put new notes in historical context https://t.co/KQAGXVOzvz

Just recently figured out how I wanted to handle this page in Roam, so it's a work in progress.

Timeline divided into major divisions of history. Older periods divided by century. Recent centuries divided into decades. Events mentioned in Scripture are highlighted in blue. https://t.co/HjDi38eQBV

Metaphor #4: [[Points]]

i.e. points on a timeline; turning points; plot points

Understanding historical context is fundamental to knowledge work. The study of history is a kind of general epistemology. How can we have knowledge of the past? And how does that inform the present? https://t.co/rtEuTHrKQ4

@CalebFiggers I agree. I think many people instinctively classify secondhand information as firsthand when it feels more like an “experience”.

@CalebFiggers Really? Do you think they’d agree that most of what they know came through parents, teachers, news, books, etc.?

This has always been true. But with social media, news cycles, and increased access to education, it seems clear that we rely more than ever on secondhand sources.

When you're working on a problem, think about Hickey's "hammock time".

When someone pits reason against experience, recall Kreeft's description of CSL as "romantic rationalist".

And when someone sums up an idea with a catchy phrase, claim that new "concept handle" as your own.

In Roam, I've been creating a custom lexicon by collecting and linking these terms and phrases.

They give me easy access points into my notes and fresh ways to communicate those ideas to others.

They also make great titles and headings. https://t.co/YDz7qvH5wD

Metaphor #3: [[Handles]]

A concept handle is "a memorable term or phrase that represents a complex, often abstract topic."

With a good handle, you can pull in a constellation of related ideas, arguments, examples, and sources.

https://t.co/RY1FqROBxl https://t.co/NLWlm1nBU6
@Empathy2000 “Most of us need the crutch at times; but of course it's idiotic to use the crutch when our own legs can do the journey on their own!” (CSL)

These ideas also provide an interesting set of lenses (switching up the metaphor) for thinking about a subject.

When looking for big ideas for a topic, they can reveal new questions to ask. New paths to explore.

https://t.co/RSdXsobMxp

TfTs make it easy to link notes. You can do this indiscriminately and try to find connections later.

But it's also useful to keep a curated list of these ideas. A personalized Syntopicon that can reveal inter-subject connections (cf. consilience and cross-level coherence). https://t.co/IYcHLfVOBh

Metaphor #2: [[Seeds]]

There are fundamental, universal concepts that connect human knowledge. Think Adler's ideas, Alexander's fundamental properties, or Plato's forms.

Discovered, not invented. Our minds bump into these realities just like our bodies bump into physical ones. https://t.co/OeHbaPNauN

@tombarys @cortexfutura I create new pages for permanent notes as part of that last layer. Layers 3-4 are more like literature notes so I prefer to keep them in context.

As I’m analyzing the highlights/text (Layer 3), I’m just identifying the key elements there. I still use a version of this: https://t.co/f2u63MVB6k
@m_ashcroft Have to admit that I enjoy flagging opponents in bullet/blitz though. Just flagged this guy a few minutes ago in a drawn position. https://t.co/ll2ltiye7n

@m_ashcroft Increment helps a lot with this. Top level tournaments even have 30-second increment sometimes.

Still, I much prefer to watch bullet/blitz chess online for this reason. Games in the faster time controls are not as enjoyable otb.

Some final thoughts:

1. Goal here is context-dependent insights (@cortexfutura)
2. Some texts don't require as much attention, so leave out layers as needed
3. Don't work on all layers at the same time
4. Layers should help others (or future self) retrace your thought process

Tiago Forte includes some of these layers in what he calls "progressive summarization".

In Roam, you just have options for extra layers while still preserving context.

Layers 1-2 in parent blocks
Layers 3-4 in child blocks
Layer 5 on the DNP
Layers 6-7 in the block/page refs https://t.co/RFYTFzcAMl

Metaphor #1: [[Layers]]

Whenever you engaging with the words and ideas of others, you are working on multiple layers.

Layer 1: Read
Layer 2: Annotate
Layer 3: Analyze
Layer 4: Elaborate
Layer 5: Apply
Layer 6: Connect
Layer 7: Remix https://t.co/u7Ew1k6339

I'm familiar enough with these lists now that I rarely need to refer to them as I tag notes.

If I ever need to add a tag that does not exist yet, I just make sure it's also added to one of those 5 lists.

My current solution involves 5 curated lists that I use to tag blocks/pages.

Entities (primarily from templates)
Statuses
Ideas (Custom Syntopicon)
Subjects (Personal Wiki)
Knowledge Elements

re: which tags should I use?

When I tried to write exclusively on the DNP, I went crazy with tags and links. But I felt like everything got buried in this tangle of links and DNPs.

I forgot what tags I used in the past, and I didn't have a good way to keep track of them.

re: why is search so bad?

I frequently search my notes, and I really disliked the original search option in Roam.

Seems like there is a lot of room for improvement here. Perhaps LLMs can help in the future?

For now, this new search view component is a huge improvement. https://t.co/e6NYWNZ95e

My current strategy is to add notes to 30-40 top-level pages. As the pages grow, I copy sections to separate pages and link to them on that top-level page.

Note that this doesn't have to happen until the page gets very large.

e.g. I have a page that just passed 90,000 words.

re: where do I put stuff? how do I find it?

One strategy is to just put it all on the DNPs. I am still experimenting with this in one of my graphs, but sadly, I haven't been able to get it to work for elaborating, connecting, and synthesizing ideas over time.

I've asked all of these questions (except the load time one). Still not sure I have good answers.

Just going to riff on a few of these issues and mention my current (tentative) solutions. https://t.co/PIWLTjZWEW

Roam blocks and outliner help me to avoid task/project confusion.

Key is to avoid big tasks. So an easy [[Pattern]] is to break down every task into smaller tasks.

If I can't finish everything that day, I tag the parent block with a future date and pick up where I left off.

One [[Benefit]] I noticed almost immediately is that interstitial journaling (IJ) closes loops that would normally stay open for me until the end of the day. 

Even in the middle of a chaotic day, timestamping each unit of work means I don't feel the weight of everything all day.

7. Structure and Form

Maybe a stretch but I'm thinking of Plato's dialogues. Or Aquinas' article format in both "Summa contra Gentiles" and later in "Summa Theologiae".

Like versions of a video game having the same structure but different storylines, quests, even characters.

6. New Frames

Author adds or revises a prologue, preface, or introduction.

For example, C.S. Lewis added a preface to later editions of "The Screwtape Letters" addressing criticisms and answering some common questions.

He also added a new chapter: "Screwtape Proposes a Toast". https://t.co/8ajQvB9VrN

5. Thematic Development

Alexander's works (from the op) are a wonderful example of this.

Friend told me this is also true for Campbell's "Masks of God" series and "Hero with a Thousand Faces".

The seed of an early work is used to grow something new but qualitatively the same.

4. Genre Translation

"That Hideous Strength" is "Abolition of Man" in fiction form. Same with Orwell’s "1984" and his "Politics and the English Language" essay.

It's common to translate into other media (e.g. movies). But I love the idea of using other written genres. https://t.co/8bIiDbbuOY

3. Expanded Thesis

"Matter with Things" develops McGilchrist's original thesis in "Master and his Emissary" into a much more comprehensive work.

By the way, even if "Master and his Emissary 2" was an accurate title, I think you'd probably reject it for marketing reasons. https://t.co/SaE7TE0Deo

2. Personal Retrospective

Augustine wrote "Reconsiderations", a retrospective re-reading and review of each of his works (retractions, updated opinions, etc.)

In Chapter 3 ("Why I Write Such Excellent Books") of "Ecce Homo", Nietzsche does something similar for his books. https://t.co/ZoxEYP2x8R

1. Updated Editions

Darwin's "Origin of Species" changed significantly over time. Fun fact: the "survival of the fittest" phrase wasn't added until the 5th ed.

This kind of extensive revision in scientific or theoretical books is usually a response to critiques or new evidence. https://t.co/fJ1ep4tUWR

Just thinking about the different ways authors remix, rewrite, or revisit their ideas.

N.T. Wright on the four components of a [[worldview]].

https://t.co/5cDCJYHcNy

Second, these features are treated separately here, but in reality, they overlap in many interesting ways.

A few examples: Myth combines story and symbol. Ritual combines symbol and praxis. History combines narrative and belief. Habit combines belief and praxis.

A couple observations:

First, notice that these worldview features are enacted (#3 and #4), not just propositional (#1 and #2). Practical not just theoretical.

Put another way, there are four things worldviews do for us:

1. They provide stories through which we view reality
2. They answer basic questions of human existence
3. They come to expression in cultural symbols (artifacts and events)
4. They entail a "way-of-being-in-the-world"

Wright suggests we study worldviews by considering those four features:

1. Characteristic stories
2. Foundational beliefs (answers to fundamental questions)
3. Fundamental symbols, and
4. Habitual praxis

"Worldviews are the basic stuff of human existence, the lens through which the world is seen, the blueprint for how one should live in it, and above all the sense of identity and place which enables human beings to be what they are." - N.T. Wright

@fortelabs Two or more of the following:

1. Transformative insights/ideas
2. Planning to re-read
3. Would recommend to anyone
4. Striking writing style or perspective

Step 🔟: References and bibliography

Use the appropriate reference format to credit authors and sources that you used to write your essay.

Read JBP's full "Essay Writing Guide" here: https://t.co/3lhBbNglDO

Check out the app built to implement these ideas here: @essay_app

Step 9⃣: Repeat steps as necessary

To further improve your essay, wait a few days and repeat the process of re-writing, re-ordering, and re-outlining.

How do you know when you are done revising? When you re-write sentences, and you're not sure if the new ones are better.

Once you've finished writing the new outline, cut and paste the sentences and/or paragraphs from your first draft into this new structure.

Leave out anything that is unnecessary.

Step 8⃣: Generate a new outline

Write a new outline of 10-15 sentences. Don’t look at your essay while you do this.

Force yourself to reconstruct your argument from memory, and you will likely improve and simplify it. Your memory can be a great filter for what is essential.

Step 7⃣: Structure your essay

Arrange your new and improved paragraphs as individual units. Check to make sure they are in logical order.

Review your original outline. It may be that your initial essay structure needs to be updated

Read each sentence aloud. If it sounds awkward, rewrite it again. Try editing out loud (maybe by talking to a friend).

Once you've finished re-writing a sentence, read all the versions and choose the best one.

Check that sentences are in the right order.

Edit your draft one paragraph at a time.

Place each sentence on its own line. Then write a second version of each sentence under the first one.

Try to make each sentence better in the second version. "Better" primarily meaning more simple and concise.

Step 6⃣: Edit your draft

The drafting process is all about producing (divergence). The editing process is all about reducing and arranging (convergence).

Tackle them in two different steps. Trying to accomplish both at the same time is always counterproductive.

Step 5⃣: Produce your first draft

Use your outline and notes to write a paragraph (10-15 sentences) for each outline point. If you get stuck, just move on to the next point.

Don't worry about grammar and sentence structure yet. Just get the rough ideas down.

Step 4⃣: Write your outline

The outline is the skeleton of your essay. It's the distillation of your argument. 

The outline points will become topic sentences for your paragraphs.

At this level of resolution, you should be able to see if your essay is coherent and interesting.

You will need 2-3 times as many words in your notes as you will need for your essay.

Why? Because you should know more about your topic than you include in your essay. You need to keep what is good, so you need to have enough words to throw away what’s bad.

If you struggle with this kind of note-taking, try this exercise: "Read a paragraph. Look away. Then say to yourself, out loud... what the paragraph meant.”

Listen (or record) what you say and write that down.

Your notes are the conversation you have with the writer.

Summarize their ideas to check understanding.

If you don't understand or agree with what you read, write down why. Ask questions. Expect good authors to answer them later.

Step 3⃣: Take notes as you read

When taking notes, don’t bother too much with highlighting, underlining, or direct quotes.

Your notes should reflect what you have learned (summaries) or what you don't understand yet (questions).

Step 2⃣: Create a reading list

Writer's block is just a sign that you haven't read enough. If you have nothing to say, you need to read more (and more widely).

Chase the footnotes on great sources and writers.

Assume you'll need 5-10 sources for every 1,000 words of essay.

Now on to the essay writing steps.

Step 1⃣: Select a topic question

If you have already selected a topic, write 10 potentially interesting topic questions.

If you do not have a topic or if you cannot write 10 questions, skip ahead to Step 2 and return to this step afterwards.

First a few rules to follow (or break at your own risk):

1. A paragraph should have 1 idea and 10 sentences
2. Don't be boring. Technical skill can't overcome a lack of originality & creativity.
3. Don't underestimate aesthetics (beauty & elegance).
4. Finished beats perfect.

Like any written work, an essay simultaneously exists at multiple levels of resolution:

- Word
- Sentence
- Paragraph
- Paragraph Order (outline)
- Essay
- Reader Context
- Cultural Context

For an essay to succeed, it must work at all these levels. A difficult but worthy goal.
An essay is a "short piece written by someone attempting to explore a topic or answer a question". It allows a writer to organize and express ideas in a clear and compelling way.

Is beauty really just in the eye of the beholder?
Is love just chemicals reacting in the brain?
Is justice just a flexible word for what those in power get away with?

Or is the nature of these ideas (and others) determined by something outside of us? 8/

Are you skeptical about a pure, ideal essence of mud, dust, or cups? I get it.

What about Justice? Beauty? Love? 

I'm not convinced anyone really believes these ideas are entirely subjective. 7/

Of course, a lot of the debate has been theoretical.

But the practical problem persists. Our minds bump into abstract realities just like our bodies bump into physical ones.

They really do seem to be as real as rocks. They might be more real than rocks. 6/

In the 14th century, William of Occam (and his nominalism) effectively called off the search, announcing that (surprise!) the ideas were only in our heads the whole time.

They are subjective, not objective. Particulars, not universals. We don’t discover them; we invent them. 5/

Later, Augustine suggested a new location for the ideas: the Mind of God. 

A logical move since ideas do seem to require a mind. And if these ideas are indeed eternal and unchanging, it makes sense that they would live in the mind of the eternal, unchanging God. 4/

Aristotle gave Plato's ideas a new metaphysical address. He said they exist in physical objects or persons themselves. 

So your shirt doesn't take its shade from the perfect essence of redness in the sky. Instead that perfect redness inhabits your shirt (and others like it). 3/

Plato argued for a world of these ideas above this reality. 

Imagine a pristine museum in the sky where the perfect forms of justice, cupness, and redness live on tidy shelves that are never dusty. Except (of course) for the shelf with the pure form of dustiness. 2/

Thesis: There are fundamental, universal concepts connecting everything you read, think, see, and do. These concepts (ideas) are the essence of human knowledge.

Philosophers spent a few millennia debating this ancient theory. Mostly asking, “Where do these ideas come from?” 1/

In @RoamResearch, the best way to reinforce these rhythms is through the daily notes page (DNP):

1. Create templates for specific days of the year or week
2. Use extensions like Automatic DNP or the SRS feature in Date UX Improvements
3. Manually link blocks to a future dates

Unlike [[Patterns]] which are prompted by a problem or goal in a specific context, rhythms are broader and more personal.

You are making notes to your future self. Reminders to focus your attention on what is persistently meaningful. Important even when it's not urgent.

I first noticed this modeled in religious works like the Book of Common Prayer and the Rule of Saint Benedict.

Modern books on rituals, traditions, and habits are working with the same basic principles.

I was also inspired by specific examples in "Every Moment Holy" (McKelvey).

Metaphor #8: [[Rhythms]]

7. I shall sometimes look back at the freshness of vision I had in childhood and try, at least for a little while, to be, in the words of Lewis Carroll, the “child of the pure unclouded brow, and dreaming eyes of wonder.

6. I shall open my eyes and ears. I shall simply stare at a tree, a flower, a cloud, or a person. I shall not be concerned to ask what they are but simply be glad that they are. I shall joyfully allow them the mystery of their “divine, magical, terrifying and ecstatic” existence.

5. I shall not demean my own uniqueness by envy of others. I shall stop boring into myself to discover what psychological or social categories I might belong to. Mostly I shall simply forget about myself and do my work.

@Jeanvaljean689 @MikeKra36812131 Right. And in the spirit of Adler’s ideas, my list of [[Concepts]] in Roam is carefully curated. I am very slow to add to that list.

They’re not just subjects or topics. They’re more fundamental than that. I want them to be relevant across many different domains and sources.

@Fis4Thought @RoamResearch I revisit based on the knowledge work I feel like doing that day.

If I want to analyze ideas, I go to [[Proposition]] and [[Argument]].

If I want to review what I've learned, I go to [[Question]].

And if I just want to write, I choose a subject and collect examples from each.

@manunamz Yes, there are sources for the 12 main elements.

Concept: Syntopicon (Adler)
Term: "Concept Handles" (Alexander)
Argument: Logic (Kreeft)
Practice: Pattern Language (Alexander)
Annotation: Ahrens/Luhmann/Adler
Structure: @francis_miller

There are others. I should make a list.
It’s still early. But I feel like there will be compound interest from this simple taxonomy of knowledge elements in years to come. https://t.co/yQuIWtRryH

I’ve also been using templates to help guide what should be nested under each type of block.

Terms require definitions.
Arguments require assumptions, evidence, sources, objections, etc.
Practices normally include problem, solution, examples, steps, etc.

For example, I can create a personalized catechism for Romans by filtering the #[[Question]] blocks from that commentary page.

“The way to get over creative block is to simply place some constraints on yourself. It seems contradictory, but when it comes to creative work, limitations mean freedom.” - @austinkleon

And it was a great lesson on the value of constraints in creativity. 

Instead of staring at a blank page each week or waiting for inspiration to strike, I started with a list of possible constraints. 

Then, I selected the one that gave me the inspiration I needed that week.

1 storyline. 3 months. 12 writing genres and document types.

DISCLAIMER: No analogy is perfect. This is especially true for one that attempts to represent the complexity of multiple fields of study (epistemology, philosophy, theology, and apologetics).

So here are a few notes on the limits of this analogy.

5/5 https://t.co/rdoUeWPVx3

There are 3 main ways to evaluate a worldview:

1. Test the evidence that is presented (or missing).
2. Examine the evidence to see if it supports your beliefs.
3. Check the evidence and beliefs for coherence (e.g. "Are they both supported by your worldview?")

4/5 https://t.co/vm9o6H9cXO

Your worldview is like a container for your evidence and beliefs.

Possible evidence (or "clues") includes reason, experience, testimony, memory, etc. 

Beliefs (excluding basic beliefs) are typically inferred from evidence via argument (deduction, induction, or abduction).

3/5 https://t.co/7JORk7zZPA

A worldview is a set of foundational beliefs about reality, being, nature, knowledge, and value. Normally, these beliefs are assumed, not defended.

Your answers to some key foundational questions set the boundaries for what counts as evidence and what supports your beliefs.

For me, Roam is a thinking environment for isolating, analyzing, and connecting these blocks.

With that collection of blocks, you can start building. That's where the compound interest kicks in.

And the best part is that we're still early in this story.

https://t.co/9zaQxymmlz

You can even create templates to help support these block types.

Claims require assumptions, evidence, and sources
Terms require definitions, etymology, and references.
Patterns require problem, solution, examples, steps, etc.
References require source type, ToC, key ideas, etc.

The blocks in Roam are the perfect solution to these problems.

Almost everything is built to enhance the power of those fundamental units: block refs, in-line references, focus on block, etc.

So in Roam, the "notes" are really the blocks, not the pages.

In typical note-taking apps, you're encouraged to write paragraphs. Line after line of text.

You can try to add only one idea (block) to each note, but the process is slow and tedious.

And there's no easy way to view many notes at once or to combine them to make something new.

Now, let's go back to Roam's block-based architecture.

The problem with other note-taking tools (Evernote, Google Docs, etc.) is that they're really designed for creating knowledge structures, not for collecting the individual blocks needed to build those structures.

The more you break down these knowledge structures, the easier it is to:

a) analyze and evaluate that information
b) allocate resources to solve difficult problems and answer questions that require experience/expertise
c) remix that knowledge in new and interesting ways

Then you have to determine the specific criteria for evaluating that block type.

e.g.
Claims are evaluated on evidence, source reliability, and missing context.
Terms are evaluated on clear definitions and use cases.
Resources are evaluated on accessibility and affordability.

The key is that for each of the knowledge blocks you identify, you need to determine the criteria for evaluation.

First, you have determine whether you're evaluating for usefulness or truthfulness. Or both.

https://t.co/DpV6NpaAyT

But in practice, the list changes based on your context, goals, and sources

When I created a handbook in Roam for onboarding new project managers at our company, I added 3 additional block types that were needed for that specific structure.

Agendas
Deliverables
Milestones
As I continued to apply this rubric in new contexts and with new sources, I refined the list to include the knowledge blocks that were more universal and relevant. https://t.co/zxdWE2WKXw

These knowledge blocks (or "icons of thought") are the fundamental units of all knowledge work.

A few months ago, I created a map to trace how these blocks went from the original source to my own writing (in my own words).

https://t.co/CMdLXhl6gU

Every knowledge structure can be broken down like this.

Catechism = questions, answers, and references.
Position papers = evidence, arguments, and affirmations/denials.
Wikipedia Page = key terms, domains of inquiry, notable people, etc.
Papers = abstract, methods, results, etc.

4. Niklas Luhmann's Zettelkasten

The 90,000+ notes in Luhmann's ZK (I & II) are just another example of a knowledge structure.

They're more concise and interconnected, but they have the typical info: terms, propositions, and references.

They're more like structures than blocks

3. The Summa Theologica by Thomas Aquinas

The 3,125 theological articles in Aquinas' work contain propositions, objections, arguments, references to authority, questions, and replies.

https://t.co/H9UMsUDV7m

2. Christopher Alexander's "A Pattern Language"

The 263 architectural patterns in the book contain images, background context, evidence, problems/solutions, diagrams, cross-references, etc.

https://t.co/Nv0A8T6a3D

1. Mortimer Adlers' Syntopicon

The 102 chapters in the Syntopicon contain quotations from the Great Books, commentary from Adler, outlines of topics, references, etc.

https://t.co/i89T0uCdwi

This is the heart of all knowledge work.

Knowledge STRUCTURES are broken down into BLOCKS. Then, those blocks are analyzed, summarized, connected, and remixed to create new structures.

This is true for any field of study, but I'll illustrate with four of my favorite examples.

At its core, knowledge work requires three skills:

1. Break down a source into discrete parts
2. Evaluate those parts and identify which ones are important for your thinking and writing.
3. Recombine them into new structures that facilitate learning, transfer, and collaboration.

Side note: the classic theological disciplines are roughly aligned with those levels: Exegesis, Systematic Theology, Apologetics, Historical Theology, Practical Theology, Liturgy, etc.

Which makes sense given the Christian emphasis on worldview.

I try to “transpose” concepts across these levels:

1. Concept
2. Key Terms (engaging glossary, not just technical)
3. Catechesis (Q&A’s with answers as claims)
4. Syllogisms (built with claims)
5. Narrative (History)
6. Praxis (Habits/Lifestyle)
7. Symbol (Art, Ceremony, etc.)

Haidt suggests a model with coherence on three levels - physical, psychological, sociocultural.

If knowledge can be “analyzed at multiple levels, a special kind of coherence occurs when the levels mesh and mutually interlock.”

I like to think of it as [[Presence of Mind]]. https://t.co/PsLloXsEJ8

The most fundamental unit of knowledge is probably a concept (e.g. Adler’s ideas, Plato’s forms, etc.).

But useful academic unit? I’d suggest something involving “cross-level coherence” (HT: Haidt).

Kimbro's answer: Information > Knowledge > Wisdom

There isn't really a complete list, but throughout the book, the author identifies about 40 of the thought patterns he uses to identify the ideas he adds to his notes.

e.g. Problem, Question, Possible Action, Model, Boundary, Observation, Warning, Definition, Rule, etc.

Robert Horn is known for trying to identify patterns like this in technical documentation:

https://t.co/JR6XvyCa29

But in individual (personal) reading, writing, thinking, and problem-solving, the patterns are different.

Most interesting takeaway for me was the [[Icons of Thought]] that Kimbro uses to identify the different "elements" of his thinking.

Your thinking process may seem chaotic, but if you pay attention, you'll notice patterns.

These "icons of thought" help identify these patterns.

The section on "thought moods" reminded me a lot of the "mind like water" idea (GTD). Only for knowledge work.

If you habitually throw out good ideas, your mind stops giving solutions. But in a trusted system, the mind gets happy about solving problems and gives more pieces.

I know there are differences here, but I think there are interesting connections to the Zettelkasten system too.

"Speed Thoughts" = Fleeting Notes
"Reference" = Literature Notes
"Points of Interest" = Permanent Notes

Add to that the emphasis on elaboration and connecting ideas.

@brandontoner @visakanv For Lewis, this even carried over to his non-fiction. "That Hideous Strength" is basically just "Abolition of Man" in fictional form.

@brandontoner @visakanv To use visa's metaphor, they lived in the city they were writing about, and they never stopped exploring it.

But when they discovered something new, they didn't have to work as hard to remember it, because it was already connected to everything else.

@brandontoner @visakanv Owen Barfield on Lewis: "There was something in the whole quality and structure of his thinking, something for which the best label I can find is ‘presence of mind'... somehow what he thought about everything was secretly present in what he thought about anything.”

@brandontoner @visakanv I've noticed this with both Lewis and Tolkien. I think part of the "sorcery" is the vibrancy and completeness of their worldview.

They weren't just writing about a world. They were writing from within one. And I'm talking about more than the usual world-building features.

🔟 Write a mission statement for yourself as a writer. 

What is most interesting and exceptional about your voice? What are the subjects you’re most drawn to in writing? 

Knowing who you are as a writer is the first step to being a good writer.

Revise your statement as needed.

9️⃣ Use jargon as a writing prompt for an essay. 

Dive into an unfamiliar vocation, hobby, or niche group. Explore the jargon of that subject. It’s really just shorthand. 

Choose an example of jargon that interests you and use it as a launching point for your essay.

8️⃣ Use a creative title as a writing prompt. 

Find a descriptive title. Don’t worry about where it will take you. Just choose one that resonated with you from a billboard, book cover, etc. 

Now work backwards: What is the essence of the story you’re telling? What is its theme?

7️⃣ Co-write an article using an email conversation. 

Find a writer to swap emails with. Pick a topic but limit yourselves to 1 paragraph written in less than 15 minutes.

Pay attention to how the conversation turns. Did your tone or opinions change? What ideas stood out to you?

6️⃣ Develop your writing style through genre translation. 

Your writing style is an instrument that needs to be honed and trained.

Try writing a short vignette. Then, rewrite it as an email, a series of texts, a letter to a friend, and something published in a literary magazine.

5️⃣ Use Wikipedia for rabbit trail research.

Click on a random Wikipedia page and write down any information that seems interesting.

Identify the most compelling characters and most surprising details for your story. Click to related pages to fill out the rest of the story.

4️⃣ Practice your non-fiction scene-setting and world-building.

Go to the house of someone you know and write a profile of that person using only what you see. 

How well can you summon this person by describing this most intimate space? Did you capture them? What did you miss?

3️⃣ Visit a library and brainstorm ideas using physical shelves. 

What you really want is surprising connections and discoveries. Search engines don’t usually give you that. Library shelves often do. 

Find a book on your topic. Then, search the shelves around it for ideas.

2️⃣ Use conversations to test your ideas and get real time feedback.

Is the other person engaged? Confused? Surprised?

Did they suggest resources, related topics, or references that you can use to further your ideas.

1️⃣ Create an archipelago of ideas before organizing your essay.

You don't have all the pieces, so you don’t know how they should fit together. Don’t waste time on transitions that may be deleted/edited later. 

Just start writing and number the sections as you go.

We've had the yard sale. Moved our stuff to storage. Spent 50+ hours on home repair projects. Plodded through all the luggage and travel requirements.

Not crazy at all... right?

But I think it's the good kind of crazy. The kind that feels a bit like performance art.

We're not pretending to be someone we're not. We're just exploring a new version of our family.

In a dozen new places. With lots of new people.

So if you asked Alexander to identify the patterns in a room, I don't think he'd point to tables and chairs.

This is what awaits you in the stories of Wodehouse. If you're new to his books, here are my suggestions: for a full novel, try "Code of the Woosters" or "Leave it to Psmith". For short stories, you can't go wrong with Jeeves. My favorite is "Carry On, Jeeves".

The joys of reading Wodehouse are many. Comic misunderstandings, acrobatic wordplay, surprising metaphors, and hilarious dialogue. It's great fun. I recall a coffee shop visit where I desperately tried (& failed) to hold back laughter and tears as I read "Uncle Fred Flits By".

In his book, "Writers to Read", @douglaswils says that one of the besetting sins of our age is our inability to make a point (even a good one) without sounding shrill. Wodehouse can help us wield words with joy and wit, not anger and disdain.

Evelyn Waugh said of Wodehouse: "[His] idyllic world can never stale. He will continue to release future generations from captivity that may be more irksome than our own. He has made a world for us to live in and delight in.”

The metaphors are surprising, memorable, and consistently hilarious. Aristotle believed that facility with metaphor was a mark of genius. If this is true (and I believe it is), we have a lot to learn from the genius of Wodehouse.

2) "Some minds are like soup in a poor restaurant - better left unstirred.” 3) "She came leaping towards me, like Lady Macbeth coming to get first-hand news from the guest-room." 4) "He looked like a sheep with a secret sorrow."

But the true genius of Wodehouse is his mastery of metaphor.  A few examples will have to suffice here: 1) "He had the look of one who had drunk the cup of life and found a dead beetle at the bottom."

Though I suspect he never won any Scripture Knowledge Awards as a child, he was obviously very familiar with the Bible. As one author put it, "he could nail down a biblical allusion as quickly as Jael, the wife of Heber."

A Wodehouse plot is usually outrageous and a bit tangled. His characters are all caricatures, but even their eccentricities tell the truth about human nature. They have simple desires (usually involving relationships or avoiding trouble), which makes them immediately relatable.

But he was also modest when asked about his success: "Ask the first ten men you meet, 'Have you ever heard of P.G. Wodehouse?' and nine of them will answer 'No.' The tenth, being hard of hearing, will say, 'Down the passage, first door to the right.'"

He once said, “I never want to see anyone, and I never want to go anywhere or do anything. I just want to write.” And that's what he did. In 90 years, he wrote more than 90 books. He was a successful author, Hollywood screenwriter, and master of comic short story.

On this day in 1881, P.G. Wodehouse was born. As a child, he struggled to say his name (he later called it a "frightful label"). All he could manage was "Plum" (his version of Pelham), which was what his friends called him. This is a short introduction to his work.

“How you engage” with a book is far more important than “what you do” with it. Engaging with good content/characters is transformative. Makes you a better parent, friend, citizen, etc. And it does this even when you don’t immediately have something “interesting” to show for it.

One more thing: the modern obsession with output is really unhealthy for reading. Books become a means to an end, mined only for “useful” material.  It’s a breeding ground for confirmation bias, and it almost guarantees that your output will be shallow and half-baked.

Most people should read a lot more books. 100/year would be an amazing goal. Of course, some books should be processed slowly (take notes, add to knowledge base, etc.) and used for output. But that’s not the only (or even most important) reason to read.

And read for pleasure and on a whim. Both of these things are essential parts of a healthy reading habit. One thing is certain. If your only goal for reading is “efficiency”, you won’t read 100 books a year. And you probably won’t enjoy the books you do read.

The most formative years of your life were the first 5. And you can’t remember most of it. You certainly couldn’t pass a test on it. You‘ve been radically shaped by countless things that you‘ve forgotten. So don’t stress about efficiency or output. Just read voraciously.

If you read with the goal of wringing every good idea out of every chapter, you’ll slow down to an impossibly tedious pace. Instead, read like someone who will forget most of what you read. Because you will. No matter how many books you read.

This really terrible advice. Don’t read like you’re cramming for a test or gathering material for a research paper. Pragmatic reading assumes you know what you need to know and only benefit from it when you remember it. Neither is true. https://t.co/SVrQuLEkOA

13. Recently, I also created an [[Anti-Chrestomathy]] page for quotes that express terrible ideas, logical fallacies, or outright deceptions. Scanning this page in my database is a sober reminder to write with clarity, integrity, and humility.

12. I try to avoid copying direct quotes, because it tempts me to skip the vital work of summarizing and elaborating. But I always have a few direct quotes. These get added to my [[Chrestomathy]]. I use block references to add quotes to any relevant permanent notes.

11. For each Permanent Note, I also add links to related notes. This is where the magic happens in Roam. Adding linked notes is about creating thought trails that you can re-discover later and continue the journey. They also help me uncover surprising connections along the way.

10. One super helpful exercise involves translating one idea into as many of these knowledge types as possible. Alternatively, I may take some times to search my database for related notes that fall into each of the other knowledge types.

9. Each Permanent Note also gets tagged with one of 10 [[Knowledge Types]]: Term, Axiom,  Proposition, Question, Narrative, Symbol/Metaphor, Practice, Structure, Resource, or Commentary. Adding these knowledge types has had a massive impact on how I elaborate on ideas. https://t.co/PRuGVBTxh6

8. Every permanent note gets tagged with topics that I'm interested in studying and developing. Never more than one or two top-level, topic tags. Most tags are focused on specific contexts or current interests - how I want to use it for output in the future.

7. Each key idea is distilled into one simple sentence, which becomes the title of the Permanent Note. I add bullet points under the title, elaborating on the idea. Then, I finish with questions or leads to help me elaborate further when I return to the note. https://t.co/MYS0FifBCP

6. A few days later, I return to the literature notes and decide which ideas are important to my thinking and writing. Those ideas are used to create [[Permanent Notes]] (also called evergreen notes). Then comes the real work - not just taking notes, but processing them.

5. Next, I create [[Literature Notes]] for each source. On this new note, key ideas are summarized in my own words. Normally, about half of my original highlights get turned into literature notes. The rest stay on the source note and (this is key) are still searchable in Roam.

4. I re-read all of those notes, highlighting and bolding as I go. I sometimes add quick tags to flag bullet points for how I want to remix them later or how I want to connect them to other ideas. https://t.co/dCjDJqsNGs

3. Source notes also include the main ideas of the book, summarized in my own words (3-5 bullet points). These brief summaries of the big ideas are probably the most helpful part of my current workflow. They almost always get turned into permanent notes (more on that later).

2. Highlights from a book or article get added to the bottom of unique [[Source Notes]] in Roam. These source notes include the standard meta data (Author, URL, TOC, etc.), along with links to related resources. https://t.co/NNPB5FW8Er

I use @RoamResearch for daily journaling, project/task management, and CRM. But my primary use is knowledge management. After 7 weeks, 2,500+ pages, and 500+ permanent notes, this is how I use Roam as a personal knowledge base and digital Zettelkasten. Thread 👇

@EricRWeinstein Good rule for media consumption: when a headline focuses on %, track down the raw #, and when it focuses on #, do the math and figure out the %. There are pitfalls on both sides of the statistic, but the analysis starts when you understand both parts clearly.

Thanks to @Conaw and the team at @RoamResearch for creating a tool that is becoming the center of my entire workflow and digital life.

And thanks to apostles of #roamcult for the tips and resources to make the switch to Roam easy and fun.

7. Frictionless Writing: For me, this is the real game changer. It takes seconds to create pages, add links, search the entire database, or view multiple notes at once. This means that I get to the real work of writing and thinking much faster.

6. Fluid Structure: Notes aren't trapped in folders or buried in nested outlines. All my notes are free-floating, weighted only by how often they're connected to other notes. So I never worry about where to put a note. I just tag it and let those relationships form organically.

5. Sidebar Editing: The sidebar (split-screen) is a key feature. It allows me to work with multiple drafts, synthesize ideas from multiple notes, and move knowledge around easily. And the amazing "Zenith" theme from @azlenelza lets me work with multiple notes side-by-side.

4. Bi-Directional Linking: This is the new differentiator in knowledge management apps. Pages and tags are the same in Roam. Both create two-way connections between pages/blocks. Roam also tracks my "unlinked" references, which opens up even more avenues for serendipity.

3. Overlapping Hierarchies: Roam is a tool for networked thought with nodes for each idea, but it supports hierarchies. I started with large structure notes of topics and sub-topics, but smaller structure notes have grown up organically over time. Roam handles both seamlessly.

2. Easy Search Functions: From anywhere in the app, I can search across my entire database on a block level with (()) or on a page level with [[]]. It's much faster than searching tag clouds, navigating through folders, or even using search functions in other apps.

1. Built-in Atomicity: The bullet point structure rewards brevity and clarity. These blocks are the basic units of the entire system. The more atomized my notes are, the easier they are to connect to other ideas.

One process I still can’t do well in available software is structuring talks & essays. Requires a spatial canvas that handles text well.

Final complaint; I can’t export this spatial map into anything other than a PDF or PNG.

some meta observations of the way I read and take notes in my books 

1. I underline and “sideline”, but I try not to overdo it. maybe 5% of a page or less. This is mainly to guide my attention on future rereads, and to identify phrases that resonate with me https://t.co/eNhiKsXeTJ
2. I ask questions. This is super useful for me to aggregate at the end of the read; it gives me a bunch of things to follow up on and explore https://t.co/XZ5zUUXpHC

3. when a highlight is really good, I put a ⭐️ there, and I add it to an index on a blank page at the start or end of the book. this makes it easy for me to pick up any of my books and quickly find the quotes and sections that I thought were really good https://t.co/D4xKh24tVY
4. when it feels natural, I summarize and restate the author’s ideas in my own words. some of this phrasing ends up popping up in my tweets and other writing https://t.co/U8rcwiHBhJ
5. if I read a book multiple times I try to use a different colored pen each time https://t.co/6vLG7ys4wO

It's difficult to express how explosive this idea was to me at the time (and is still). By way of understatement, infer what you will from the question I added to the margin:

"Doesn't this totally redefine the foundation of all ethics from 'What God says' to 'Who God is'?"

Dear reader, that is exactly what that does.

The implications are (and were) literally, physically staggering.

This idea gives the backstop for why obeying God's commands is the right thing to do. It's not because God is a cosmic bully who will beat up your soul forever if you disobey.

It's that literally EXISTENCE ITSELF is designed to accord with something greater than itself.

Let me tell you a quick story to illustrate why you should pay attention to the tools you're using. Let's call this thread The Magic of Categorically Better Tools.

Recently, my wife and I moved to a new town.

This applies, of course, to software as well. And I won't surprise you when I say that @RoamResearch is the hammer drill to Notion or Evernote's impact drill. It's a _categorically better tool_ for writing and thinking.

Roam makes drilling into hard problems a breeze and actually enjoyable. I've been using it for 15, 16 months now and that still holds true. @RoamResearch is a hammer drill for intellectual problems. Don't miss out on using it.

"Love the Lord and love your neighbor" is not good news—not for sinners like us, who are enslaved by sin to loving ourselves more than anyone else.

Apart from Jesus, those two simple commands justly condemn us. Why? Because, as simple as they are, we do not keep them.

The gospel is this good news: Jesus offers sinners who repent and believe something they do not deserve and cannot earn themselves—in fact, he offers them the exact opposite of what they've earned—namely, restored relationship with God himself and eternal life with him forever.

I saw someone on this bird app equate "Love the Lord and love your neighbor" with "the gospel."

Those commands are not the gospel. They are a summary of the OT law.

The gospel is that Jesus kept those commands on our behalf, and then took on our punishment for breaking them.

You can quote tweet an idea (a single tweet)

You can make "threads of threads"...

If you do creative knowledge work, you need a system.

In 2018 I learned a massively useful system to go from collecting notes to creating shareable insights.

The CODE Framework by @fortelabs:
- Collect
- Organize
- Distill
- Express

🧵 What it is and how I use it to create 👇

"Creativity is just connecting things." —Steve Jobs

Creative people are better at seeing the relationships between ideas, which is a crucial skill in our complex world.

Becoming a better note-taker and note-maker, you become a more creative thinker as you start to see patterns.

"A digital note is a 'knowledge building block':

A discrete unit of information interpreted through your unique perspective and stored outside your head." —@fortelabs 

Like a Lego block, a knowledge block stands on its own and has value.

At the core of the CODE Framework is your note-taking app. Why so?

A note-taking app enables you to:

- See the connections between information.

- Interact with visual pieces of information.

-Get the raw materials for insights and perspectives.

- Let ideas simmer over time.

The spice must flow because oil must flow. Without oil (i.e. transportation energy) civilisation collapses.

That is the central fact of the Dune galactic economy.

By downplaying that Paul's leverage and Paul's true power disappears completely.

Because Villeneuve opted to leave out the Guild Navigators the equivalence of spice with oil is lost.

Paul's threat to destroy the spice fields is equivalent to the destruction of all sources of oil in our world.

It would bring on a Dark Age.

I'm in two minds about Dune Part 2.

On the one hand it is a cinematic masterpiece that is genuinely moving.

On the other hand it left out a crucial element from the book that gives the ending its terrifying power: the equation that Spice = Oil.

The movie fails to impress that.

This feeling drives me nuts!

Mostly because I sense that it could be fixed. 

What context would I need to trigger memory of the “moment-of-saving”?

If I can remember the moment-of-saving, I can reconstruct most of the meaningful context!

Once the portal opens, you can start to play with that energetic continuum across time. 

You engage in an interaction with your future state. 

A master of context can distill a moment into its essential resonance — building the portal.

It’s like traveling back in time. 

The payoff of being able to vividly return to a thought space or state of mind is huuuuge.

Those moments of resonance hold immense power. 

I want to be able to jump between them — like a time traveller. Revisiting the energy of a moment.

If I can revisit the source — the moment — the visual field — emotion — or any other markers of the thought space, I can recall the rest of the details quite vividly. 

Context matters.

Then if I can link to the WHY that moment or thought-space was resonant. That’s magic.

The idea of *context preservation* has been SO persistent for me over the past while.

It’s amazing how powerful context is. It’s the trigger that allows the reconstruction of a thought-space. A state of mind.

My goal with one-pagers:

• Curate the 10 best resources to learn the 80/20 of what matters 

• Curate the 5-10 Twitter accounts to follow 

• Break the space into ~7 core frameworks/buckets

• Identify at least 5 pieces of commonly-given advice that beginners should ignore

An idea I'm exploring: creating learning groups.

Any time I want to learn something, I'm going to find 3 or 4 others who want to learn it as well. 

Starting going down the ETH rabbit-hole with @businessbarista and @RomeenSheth
And on top of this, I’m building a learning group around the topic to, again, accelerate the learning feedback loop. https://t.co/9VcofNXZ1H

You can copy+paste this exact framework to learn just about anything.

• Pick a topic
• Outline a one-pager
• Curate some resources
• Form a learning group to do it with you 

Then, just start reading, listening, and asking questions. 

And doing it in public = free upside.

The fastest way to go from a white belt (level 0) to a yellow belt (level 4):

Create a one-page primer on the subject and share it others. 

This is the perfect mix of public accountability + level of difficulty to speed up the learning process.

- a page with all mentions of a concept (#defi)

21. Tags are a good idea because they allow you to create those overlapping collections. They are great for browsing, and that is a genuine source of insight. They are bad because they are flat (and difficult to merge together) & require you to guess your structure ahead of time

20. Folders are a good idea -- because hierarchical organization is fundamental to human thinking -- we like categorizing things. Folders are a bad idea because every actual thing fits into multiple categories.

19. If you rely on tags, you can group the same item into multiple collections, but you lose the ability to move from a higher "strategic level" down to the more granular question -- and you end up trying to remember which of your 100 tags is useful to each concept.

All considered, @RoamResearch has absolutely changed my life and I expect to continue using it as long as I possibly can.

__fin__

- Where Roam shines most is the overlap of stream-of-consciousness capture with lazy (i.e. "when I get around to it") poly-hierarchical organization and *extremely* easy rediscovery (so long as you're [[Tag]]ing [[Absolutely]] [[Everything]], which you seriously should be)

- Also, Roam aspires to be "the MS Excel of text" but has a long way to go to reach that aspiration, if it ever will. The Query Builder extension does more for this than pretty much any native feature, IMHO.

- Roam is awesome for research and super in-depth reading.

- It's *pretty good* for short-form (<1,000 word) writing and planning out longer writing (outlining esp.).

- For full long-form writing, or any prose writing with lots of iteration/editing, Roam is too high-friction.

- Roam is super useful for a *specific kind* of task management—when you're brainstorming through a project or catching TODOs in the middle of a meeting, Roam is the GOAT. But day-to-day "Take out the trash" TODOs want a different system (I'm back to using Todoist for those)

- [[Tag]] [[Absolutely]] [[Everything]]. EVERYTHING. Don't listen to [[[[Tag]] [[Minimalist]]]]s. Embrace [[[[Tag]] [[Overload]]]].

- People's names are by far the most useful tags you'll create. ALWAYS tag names (usually just First and Last, not middle initial)

- Add inline comments using markdown link to a block reference containing comment around a highlighted speech bubble emoji, like this: ^^[💬](((blockRefHere)))^^ (AHK shortcut for this)

- Use other emojis for other things (like definitions of terms: [📖](((blockRefHere)))

- Always wrap titles of Articles/Blog Posts in quotation marks, but not books (helps to visually distinguish just from the link)

- For platforms/websites with many authors, namespace like this: [[Author Name/platform abbreviation/"Article title"]]

- Use roam/templates for as much as possible (because that means they automatically work on Mobile too)

- Autohotkey and VS Code play super important roles in conjunction with Roam itself (Autohotkey for formatting, VS Code for fast editing—editing in Roam is slow)

- Mandatory extensions: Find & Replace, Query Builder, roam hierarchy

- Extensions to avoid: Roam42 (now deprecated)/Smartblocks v1 and v2/Workbench, Discourse Graph (all are super cool, but seem not to scale well past 10k+ pages without slowing performance unacceptably)

- [[Nested [[Page [[Titles]]]]]] are awesome, use them everywhere 

- When importing text from blogs/articles/etc. break paragraphs up and indent by sentence (use regex to automate this)

- Do not create pages for articles, blog posts, or book chapters unless you have something to put on there

- DO create pages for ideas, terms, concepts EVEN IF you don't have anything to put on there (~75% of my pages are blank, but tie together referenced pages)

- Always namespace books, articles, blog posts w/ author's FULL name (when single author) and authors last names (when multiple author)

- Store books with a single page for the book, then a page each for each chapter (namespace chapters w/ book title abbreviation)

- Use the Daily Note Page for as much as possible

- Include a "Metadata" section at the top of EVERY page (I did start doing this early, but there's some pages in there from before I saw the light)

I've been going back through over the last few days and curating the ~35k pages I have created in that time.

Here's some things I wish I had known all along:

That's three years, eight months, two weeks, and four days

Category theory is goosebump-inducing https://t.co/x8jABDQJ36
@CalebFiggers Started learning category theory because of this tweet
@CalebFiggers Thank you - this is fantastic - my appetite is most definitely whetted!

@elaptics If this has whetted your appetite, I wholeheartedly recommend this playlist of Category Theory lectures given by Bartosz Milewski. I like them because Bartosz comes from a programming background, so he relates it to programming more than to math. https://t.co/XGmuGyKutj

@elaptics Thinking in Category Theoretic terms has made me a more careful and more precise thinker and given me tools to learn ALL KINDS of totally different disciplines by identifying shared patterns of thought between them.

@elaptics Using these basics, plus a short list of principles (some of which have to be broken from time to time), Category Theorists construct proofs of astonishing beauty that are often useless in and of themselves, but incredibly useful once applied back to the real world.

@elaptics Finally, and this is actually optional in some variant flavors of Category Theory, is the rule of Associativity. 

You may have heard that word before in Math class—like for example, 1 + 2 and then + 3 is the same as 1 and then 2 + 3 (either way, it's 6). https://t.co/HFQyRwUgVS

@elaptics Next up is the rule of Composition, which is like the Golden Rule of Category Theory. Some people say that the ENTIRE ESSENCE of Category Theory is just this one idea. https://t.co/r6riX8grrS

@elaptics The name "Category Theory" comes from the central thing of study, namely, Categories.

A Category is any collection of Dots and Arrows that follow a careful set of rules, starting with Identity: https://t.co/YSv6d9nKi0
@elaptics Category Theory diagrams follow consistent conventions to make sure that ideas and relationships between ideas are expressed consistently. https://t.co/LHP6HeejzX
@elaptics Category Theory diagrams express pure relationship. So they can be used to model anything that has relationships (which is everything Humans can think of!). https://t.co/Ipvz87TwC2

@elaptics But once you get used to that, you realize that lots of different kinds of math, and programming, and spoken language, and philosophy, and logical reasoning, and a hundred other fields share "shapes." Category Theory is a "bridge language" that connects them to one another. https://t.co/bQ8dyn91AS

@elaptics The "Dots" can represent any "Thing." "Arrows" represent any way of "traveling" from one "Thing" to another "Thing." This can be used to represent mathematical operations, functions in programming, or any other connection between one thing and another. https://t.co/Vd9xA8EFWw

@elaptics Category Theory concepts are written either as a string of propositions (similar to formal logic) or, much more accessibly, using diagrams of Dots and Arrows.

Once you get Dots and Arrows, you've already learned +90% of Category Theory's basics. The fun stuff builds from there. https://t.co/3PkSbKyGQF

@elaptics Happy to! Thanks for asking 🙂

Category Theory is technically a type of Math. But it doesn't deal (directly) with numbers, and it doesn't rely on impossible-to-recognize symbols. At its most basic, it is a super-simple language for expressing relationships between things.

I’ve been reading How To Take Smart Notes and finally figured out why I seemed to have a super power in college…other students would stay up late studying and memorizing and I never did, I’d just ace the quizzes and tests without the stress.

The reason I could do this is simple: the ONLY thing I cared about was learning and understanding the material.  If something didn’t help me understand the material (and cramming facts and figures never did) I didn’t do it…

What I did do is read to understand and try to write out how I would explain a difficult concept or lecture to someone else.  This forced understanding, tied the new knowledge with old knowledge, and lodged it in my long term memory.

So here is the cheat codes to learning new things:
Focus on understanding.
Try to teach the concept to others.  Write it out in your own words.  
Forget the cramming and memorizing, that’s (at best) a short term strategy.
Do 100 writing sessions

Is that you want to use those as building blocks to find Good Explanations for larger patterns in your life.

@RoamResearch By DO SCIENCE, I mean...

1) Make observations

2) Sometimes.. conduct experiments to give you new things to observe

3) Seek out "Good Explanations" that account for what you've found (and more)

4) Test explanations as new observations come in

@RoamResearch A "Good Explanation" -- leaning on @DavidDeutschOxf's term here

A) Accounts for the data

B) Makes "surprising facts" seem obvious

C) is far-reaching -- ideally explaining some things you weren't focused on when coming up with it

if you want something, consider asking for it on your own behalf

(especially rather than on behalf of impacted "others", or by asserting claims you're unable to sufficiently engage on before becoming exasperated or frustrated)

"you should do this because i am telling you it's the right thing to do" - this triggers people's epistemic defenses

"i would value you doing this" - this affords people making a pragmatic choice to simply do what you want if they can stomach the (often trivial) costs

and i suppose that if this feels like an unsatisfying compromise, perhaps what you want is for others to believe what you believe

clarifying that for yourself and others also properly situates the frame of the conversation

Current AI writing tools are a great way to mass produce writing that is intensely mid, and makes you feel dead inside. Can we use AI to help the best writers do better work?

I don't know. But let's think about the bottlenecks for good writing. https://t.co/qOtTQHHQwa

1.
The tightest constraint on writing is framing/idea formulation.

You take a large cluster of ideas and hunches and shape them into a coherent vision you can pursue in writing. This usually takes me months to do.
I hope we can leverage AI to write the craziest essays and poems and plays and novels in history. We deserve that.

4.
Something I often do when editing work is to figure out ways to build bridges between domains. I don't know exactly why this works so well, but when you are able to bring in examples from surprising places, something happens in a text. It dances.

A weaker approach that might be useful is for the LLM to do a reverse outlining, where it extracts the arguments for every paragraph and lists them. And then group the arguments and ideas; have the model ditch obvious ideas, and highlight parts that need to be developed, etc.

My wife is really good at this, but it takes a long time, so someone please automate it. Her process seems to be: read it very slowly and stop at every friction and introspect deeply about why she felt friction or why she got excited. And then she'll usually cut like 70 percent.
Anyway, someone make an LLM that helps people iterate on their ideas and find better framings. That will unlock a lot.

I really like editors with strong opinions. Most of them I never listen to, but you can sample a lot of them and find the generative ones. Better to have a hundred crazy editors than one bland Grammarly type.

Also, LLMs tend to be very passive and unopinionated. I've done some experiments prompting them to act as Socratic questioners, to help me unpack my thinking, which is better. But still not goal-directed and value-driven enough to be worth the time.

It feels plausible that you could tweak GPT-4 or Claude to do a weak version of this? The tricky thing I guess is that they tend to have very mid taste, so you would have to be really clever to prompt them so that they locate the daring part.
Maybe you could somehow use the log probabilities of the tokens, to see which parts surprise the model most?

12/

d) Claim of action: once guilt or innocence has been established, there is an additional step if the defendant is found guilty.

The judge will hand down a punishment based on sentencing guidelines and any mitigating factors.

11/

c) Claims of definition/classification: a jury or a judge will then make a decision about the competing claims of fact and interpretation in line with the details of the legislation in order to find the defendant guilty or not guilty.

10/

b) Claims of interpretation: the prosecution and the defence will present competing claims about how the facts should be interpreted.

9)

These are the different claims being used:

a) Claims of fact: the prosecution and the defence will present competing claims about whether the facts being discussed should be considered to be true or untrue.

8/

The model can be used to analyse claims made in books, articles, papers and discussions.

To show the model in action, I'll use the example of a criminal trial, which involves the prosecution and defence trying to justify opposing claims about the guilt of a defendant.

7/ https://t.co/Gqq7hixCiN

Each argument has a main claim. However that main claim will often be supported by other types of claims.

The benefit of the hexagon model is that it shows how subsidiary claims are involved in backing up the main claim.

6/

c) claims of definition/classification (how something should be defined or classified)

d) claims of interpretation (how something should be understood)

e) claims of evaluation (whether something is good or bad)

f) claims of action (what should be done).

5/

That's why I have developed the argument claim hexagon. It shows the six different claims that can be made in arguments:

a) claims of truth (what is true or false)

b) claims of causation/influence (what causes - or influences - what)

4/ https://t.co/TzgIpObOME

However I think lots of people struggle to identify and understand the underlying structure of individual arguments.

And that makes it harder to assess an argument's validity.

3/

Our lives are saturated with arguments.

Arguments help us to decide:
- what is true or false
- what causes (or influences) what
- how something should be defined or classified
- how something should be understood
- whether something is good or bad
- what should be done.

2/

I've been working on a model to help people get better at understanding, analysing and developing arguments.

Not reading Richard Weaver has consequences.
(inspired by pointed nudges from @michael_nielsen when I'd partially lost sight of this!)

The opposing impulse would be: no, linger in that space; keep pushing on the primitives; make the system as utterly transformative as possible for some small group of people. Once it’s life-changing, figure out how it might apply to more people or contexts.

Pervasive norms in the tech industry seem to oppose this principle. If you’ve got something that shows promise in some narrow context, the strong cultural impulse is to scale that to as many people/contexts as possible.

A system which seems only modestly powerful—no matter how broadly applicable—probably can’t be evolved in-place into something transformative. The primitive abstraction will need to change. Easier to do that with a highly focused context, much harder if you’ve prematurely scaled.

An analogy to Gall’s law in design: if you want a transformatively powerful environment that applies in many contexts for many people, you probably need to evolve it from an environment which had truly transformative power in some narrow context.

Once the draft is ready to share, I'll delete the paragraph titles. The readers don't need them. They served their purpose and helped me structure the idea in a clear way. Though, if the temporary titles are catchy, I'll work them into the prose or the title of the essay.

4. It makes it easier to re-structure.

It's hard to hold an entire essay in your head. But when it's compressed down into paragraph titles, you can hold the whole essay in your working memory. This makes shifting ideas way easier.

3. It lets me visualize the atomic units of my essay.

A wall of text is like a tangled knot of ideas. You can't see the division of thought. A paragraph title is like the bullet of an outline. I can navigate the draft easily, and dive into the details that need work.

2. It lets me practice compression.

How do I turn this 100-word paragraph into 3 words? By titling paragraphs, I get to practice compression a lot. I'm always asking: What is the essence of this paragraph, and what short phrase would let my future self get it instantly?

1. It forces me to inspect each paragraph.

Titling paragraphs forces me to look at each one closely. What am I trying to say here? What question is this the answer to? Is this related to the main idea?

I'll free-write until I'm stuck. Then I'll go back to inspect each block.

Even computer threads look at items in a list one at a time. They just do it quickly.

No shame in doing the same

Filter your notes for beliefs 

Map over them

Ask

-- How surprised would I be if this were false ("weight")

-- what other beliefs do I think are true because this one is *what does this imply*

-- What other beliefs, if I changed my mind about them, would change this one

In practice -- for notes 

Filter your notes for questions

Map over your open questions and say 

-- how could I reframe this question to make it easier to answer 

-- what smaller or bigger questions help me answer this one

-- how will I know I've found an answer
Map -- take a list, and a transformation function, return a new list
Filter -- take a list, and a test, return only the items of the list that meet the test criteria
Map and Filter are both built out of reduce

painful lesson over the past ~20 years of relationships: in the medium run it’s exciting to feel hype about people who seem to relate strongly in specific ways,

21. We should read more books aloud to each other! This practice all too often ends with adulthood. But it need not!

Listening to books read aloud (not audiobooks — they can be cool too — but live direct reading) is a great and special pleasure. As is reading them.

11. More books should be not-books instead. Intentionally, actively unwritten; sketched as potential books. Preemptive Borgesian self-annihilation to make space for the books that author truly should write.

(Different from "books that shouldn't exist". Plenty of those too!)

8. We should all be more aware of (& deliberately apply) the many different levels of reading that exist — the spectrum from close reading to skimming a book quickly for major themes / ideas.

Different books + different contexts demand different approaches for optimal reading.

5. The more time you spend building your antilibrary, the better you'll get at finding & learning about great books. 

And therefore: the better your antilibrary, the better books you'll read. Same time spent reading overall (hrs or books per year) becomes more valuable.

2. An antilibrary should exist in both physical form (books on your shelves; books browsed but unpurchased at your favorite bookstores) and virtual form (in digital lists; in tweets and reviews scanned; in your memory).

1. Every curious person should be a serious reader. Ever serious reader should build an antilibrary.

(Essential vocab for this thread: your antilibrary = all the books you know about but have *not* read, designating a sort of potential energy for learning / knowledge / reading)

@lukeallenmiller I know you know this, but its important - IMHO - to know that selection is the most pervasive bias. Followed only by "perspective" in reporting.

@lukeallenmiller When I speak of a "bias of selection", it is a simple and plain as watching ESPN to bias to sports, or your local news for regional coverage - those ARE biases. Selection occurs up and down the organization stack from camera angles, to reporters, to programming guidelines, etc

@lukeallenmiller "Bias-ness" is a continuum for sure. Some are far better than others in the "ideal" of objectivity. But I stand strong that objectivity is just that, ideal - not reality. It is absolutely a reasonable expectation.

DALLE-2 has a secret language. 
"Apoploe vesrreaitais" means birds. 
"Contarra ccetnxniams luryca tanniounons" means bugs or pests. 

The prompt: "Apoploe vesrreaitais eating Contarra ccetnxniams luryca tanniounons" gives images of birds eating bugs. 

A thread (1/n)🧵 https://t.co/VzWfsCFnZo

A known limitation of DALLE-2 is that it struggles with text. For example, the prompt: "Two farmers talking about vegetables, with subtitles" gives an image that appears to have gibberish text on it. 

However, the text is not as random as it initially appears... (2/n) https://t.co/B3e5qVsTKu

Some words from the DALLE-2 language can be learned and used to create absurd prompts. For example, "painting of Apoploe vesrreaitais" gives a painting of a bird. "Apoploe vesrreaitais" means to the model "something that flies" and can be used across diverse styles. (5/n) https://t.co/w73iKN4kM1
